original_abstract: Capture and recycling of CO2 into valuable chemicals such as alcohols could help mitigate its emissions into the atmosphere. Due to its inert nature, the activation of CO2 is a critical step in improving the overall reaction kinetics during its chemical conversion. Although pure gold is an inert noble metal and cannot catalyze hydrogenation reactions, it can be activated when deposited as nanoparticles on the appropriate oxide support. In this combined experimental and theoretical study, it is shown that an electronic polarization at the metal-oxide interface of Au nanoparticles anchored and stabilized on a CeO(x)/TiO2 substrate generates active centers for CO2 adsorption and its low pressure hydrogenation, leading to a higher selectivity toward methanol. This study illustrates the importance of localized electronic properties and structure in catalysis for achieving higher alcohol selectivity from CO2 hydrogenation.
humanized_abstract: One of the strategies to mitigate the emission of CO2 as greenhouse gas is its capture and subsequent utilisation in the synthesis of valuable chemicals such as alcohols. Due to its inert nature, the activation of CO2 is essential to enhance the reaction kinetics in its transformation into valuable chemicals. Although gold at the pure state is an inert noble metal and inactive as catalyst for hydrogenation reactions, it has the ability to be activated when deposited as nanoparticles on an appropriate oxide support. In this work, it was established through a combination of experimental and theoretical studies that, Au nanoparticles anchored and stabilised on the CeO(x)/TiO2 substrate are active for the CO2 hydrogenation at low pressure while they offer a higher selectivity to methanol. The concept of this study is centred on the localization of electronic properties and structure of catalysts for the CO2 hydrogenation reaction in order to have better selectivity to higher alcohols.

Although gold at the pure state is an inert noble metal and inactive as catalyst for hydrogenation reactions, it has the ability to be activated when deposited as nanoparticles on an appropriate oxide support. In this work, it was established through a combination of experimental and theoretical studies that, Au nanoparticles anchored and stabilised on the CeO(x)/TiO2 substrate are active for the CO2 hydrogenation at low pressure while they offer a higher selectivity to methanol. The concept of this study is centred on the localization of electronic properties and structure of catalysts for the CO2 hydrogenation reaction in order to have better selectivity to higher alcohols.
